title: Cow foot stew
subtitle: Jamaican braised cow foot in thick sauce
excerpt: Split and blanched cow foot sections braised for four to five hours with butter beans, scotch bonnet, spring onion, and allspice until the collagen dissolves completely and the sauce sets to a thick, lip-coating jelly. The fat is skimmed and the gelatinous texture is the point of the dish. Deeply savoury and substantial, with a sauce that solidifies in the fridge like aspic.
sourceNotes: Cow foot stew is part of the Jamaican tradition of slow-cooking collagen-rich, economical cuts that includes oxtail stew and tripe. The dish appears across the Caribbean and in African-American Southern cooking under various names. In Jamaica it is typically served as a Sunday dinner, requiring an entire morning of slow simmering. The very high collagen content of the cow foot produces the characteristic gelatinous sauce.

content.text: Cow foot requires a blanch before the main cook. Bring the sections to a boil in cold water, discard the water, and rinse the sections under cold water. This removes the initial rendered fat and impurities that would make the sauce murky and very greasy. The blanching step is not optional.

The cook time is 4 to 5 hours at a bare simmer, the lowest heat your hob will sustain. At any higher temperature the fat emulsifies into the sauce rather than rising to the top, making the final dish greasy rather than gelatinous. If you have a slow cooker, this is the ideal use for it: 8 hours on low produces perfect results.

Place the {{cow-foot}} sections in a large pot, cover with cold water, bring to a boil, and simmer for 10 minutes. Drain and rinse well. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large heavy pot over high heat. Add the blanched cow foot and brown on all sides, about 3 minutes per side. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}}, {{spring-onion}}, {{thyme}}, {{allspice}}, {{browning-sauce}}, {{black-pepper}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Stir to coat. Add the {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole) and enough water to just cover (about 1 litre).

Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest possible simmer. Cover and cook for 3.5 to 4 hours until the meat and gelatinous skin are falling from the bone and the sauce is very thick. Check every hour and add a little water if the pot is getting dry. Add the {{butter-beans}} in the last 45 minutes of cooking. Remove the scotch bonnet before serving. Serve with white rice.

items.fix: The blanch removes excess surface fat. A gentle simmer keeps the fat rising to the top rather than emulsifying into the sauce. If the sauce is greasy, refrigerate and skim the solidified fat before reheating
items.cause: Simmer too vigorous, or blanching step skipped

items.fix: The collagen needs time and low heat. Continue cooking, cow foot can take up to 5 hours. The meat should pull cleanly from the bone when done
items.cause: Simmer too vigorous or pieces too large

items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 30 minutes and increase the heat slightly to reduce. The natural gelatin from the cow foot thickens the sauce considerably on cooling
items.cause: Too much water added during cooking

content.text: Cow foot stew sits next to oxtail in Jamaican cooking. It is a long, slow Sunday dish that needs time on the stove. The cow foot is very high in collagen. That gives a sauce no other cut can match: thick, glossy, and deeply savoury, where oxtail comes close but never quite gets there. In Britain, Caribbean home cooks make it from cow foot sold at Afro-Caribbean or halal butchers.
